title: Civil Justice Reform

flags.justification: Sec.4(b) authorizes the Attorney General to issue government-wide litigation-conduct guidelines implementing Secs.1 and 3, which 'shall serve as models' for other agencies' internal guidelines, centralizing interpretive authority over the order's civil-litigation standards in the Department of Justice.

Sec.1(f)(2)'s sanctions-review process is entirely internal: motions for sanctions against government litigation counsel are reviewed by 'the sanctions officer, or his or her designee, within the litigation counsel's agency' - a same-agency, self-policing review with no external or cross-agency check specified.

Bundles three structurally distinct reform programs into a single order: litigation-conduct guidelines for DOJ and agency counsel (Sec.1), legislative- and regulatory-drafting clarity principles tied to the OMB Circular A-19/EO12291 clearance process (Sec.2), and administrative-adjudication case-management principles (Sec.3).

Sec.1(e)(1) bars expert testimony except from experts whose theories are 'widely accepted,' defined circularly as propounded 'by at least a substantial minority of the experts in the relevant field' - an undefined, unquantified evidentiary threshold effectively establishing a federal-litigation admissibility standard by executive order roughly two years before Daubert v. Merrell Dow (1993) addressed the same question judicially.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Directs federal litigation counsel to pursue pre-filing settlement notice, settlement conferences, streamlined discovery, ADR short of binding arbitration, reliability screening for expert witnesses ('widely accepted' theories only), internal sanctions review, and two-way fee-shifting offers in civil litigation; directs agencies to review proposed legislation and regulations for drafting clarity through the OMB Circular A-19 process; directs administrative tribunals toward Administrative Conference case-management best practices; and centralizes coordination and guideline-issuing authority for all three programs in the Attorney General, while barring judicial review of the government's own compliance with the order (Sec.6).

Sec.6 declares the order creates no 'right to judicial review involving the compliance or noncompliance of the United States, its agencies, its officers, or any other person with this order' - a self-insulating clause that bars courts from reviewing whether the government itself is complying with an order that otherwise governs how the government conducts litigation before those same courts.

The preamble explicitly frames the order as intended 'to provide a model for similar reforms of litigation practices in the private sector and in various states' - a direct, stated intent to shape policy well beyond the executive branch; substantial portions of its discovery, ADR, and case-management principles were echoed in the 1993 Federal Rules of Civil Procedure amendments and the broader 1990s tort-reform/litigation-reform movement.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Sec.7(b)-(d) exempts broad categories from the order's pre-filing notice and ADR/disclosure requirements - forfeiture actions, bankruptcy/insolvency proceedings, flight-risk or asset-dissipation cases, small-dollar debt collection - and then adds an open-ended catch-all: the Attorney General may exempt any further 'limited classes of cases' whenever he 'determines that providing such notice would defeat the purpose of the litigation,' with Sec.7(d) additionally granting the Attorney General standing authority to 'issue further guidance as to the scope of this order.'

notes: The most structurally significant order in this batch to date: a landmark, historically influential litigation-reform order (explicitly intended as a model for state and private practice, and echoed in the 1993 FRCP amendments) that pairs real substantive innovation - most notably a pre-Daubert expert-reliability screening standard - with a self-insulating no-judicial-review clause (Sec.6) and an open-ended Attorney-General-determined exemption catch-all (Sec.7(d)); the combination of policy ambition and self-exempting design makes this chunk's clearest CRITICAL-flag order.

title: National Industrial Security Program

flags.justification: Designates the Secretary of Defense as Executive Agent for inspecting and monitoring most contractors, licensees, and grantees with classified-information access, and empowers the Information Security Oversight Office (ISOO) Director (established under EO12356, this stream, 56.25%) to issue binding implementation directives and order changes to non-compliant agency regulations.

Sec.102(b)(4): if an agency's reports, inspections, classified-information access, or other cooperation with the ISOO Director 'would pose an exceptional national security risk,' the agency head may simply request that the National Security Council deny the Director access - and 'the Director shall not have access pending a prompt decision by the National Security Council.' A single agency can thus unilaterally trigger a block on the security-oversight Director's own inspection access to that agency's classified-information programs.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.
The Sec.102(b)(4) access-blocking trigger - 'exceptional national security risk' - is undefined, self-invoked by the very agency being reviewed, with no independent verification standard specified.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Establishes the National Industrial Security Program to unify and govern the safeguarding of classified information released to government contractors, licensees, and grantees, with the National Security Council providing overall policy direction, the ISOO Director (EO12356, this stream, 56.25%) implementing and monitoring compliance subject to an agency-invoked access-blocking mechanism (Sec.102(b)(4)), the Secretary of Defense serving as Executive Agent for most contractor inspections, and DOE/NRC/DCI expressly retaining their existing separate statutory authorities, while substantially amending the prior industrial-security order, EO10865 (this stream, 30.0%).
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Still the operative legal foundation of the U.S. government's industrial security clearance and classified-contracting system as of 2026 (administered today by the Defense Counterintelligence and Security Agency) - one of the most durable, structurally significant orders in this batch, alongside EO12674/Principles of Ethical Conduct (this stream, 50.0%) and EO12748/locality pay (this stream, 50.0%).

Sec.102(c) explicitly preserves, rather than displaces, the separate statutory authorities of the Secretary of Energy, the Nuclear Regulatory Commission (over Atomic Energy Act information), and the Director of Central Intelligence (over intelligence sources and methods, per EO12333, this stream, 37.5%) - a genuine inter-agency-cannibalization-avoidance design, distinguishing it from this stream's more common turf-consolidation findings.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

notes: One of the most enduring, still-operative orders in this entire batch - the actual legal foundation of today's federal contractor security-clearance system - yet its own oversight mechanism contains a genuine, textually explicit gap: an agency under review can unilaterally trigger a block on the ISOO Director's inspection access simply by invoking an undefined 'exceptional national security risk,' with the Director having zero access until the NSC rules. Sec.102(c)'s explicit preservation of DOE/NRC/DCI's separate authorities is a notable positive counter-example to this stream's inter-agency-cannibalization pattern.
